fuzzy_prd:
hey team, so we need to build out this terminal table rendering thing for the ride comparison CLI. basically travelers are complaining that the current output is really hard to read вҖ” prices from different providers are showing up in inconsistent formats, and when multiple cars have the same pickup time it just lists them separately which wastes screen space and looks cluttered. we want something clean and box-drawn like those fancy terminal UIs.

the renderer needs to handle two views: one for pricing comparisons and one for wait times. for pricing, it should show each option with its fare, how far the trip is, how long it takes, and whether there's any price surge happening. for wait times, it should group options that arrive at the same time together instead of repeating rows.

we talked about this in the last sprint вҖ” remember how we handled the multi-currency stuff in that billing display module? same idea here, different currencies in the same list should each show their own symbol. also the time formatting should work the same way across both views, consistent with how we did the duration display in that earlier feature.

borders should use that gray styling we agreed on. the whole thing should be a library with clean separation вҖ” not just one big file dumping everything to stdout. input comes in as a structured object and you get back a finished string. invalid inputs should fail gracefully.

one extra pass on the details the team asked for: for surge, when surgeMultiplier is exactly 1 we show the рҹҡ« symbol for the no-surge state. when surgeMultiplier is greater than 1, we show the multiplier value followed by 'x' and then the рҹҳ¬ emoji, like '2.5x рҹҳ¬' or '1.1x рҹҳ¬'. the column header there is ' рҹ’Ҙ Surgeрҹ’Ҙ ' and that column should always take 10 characters of inner width.

for fare ranges, the format is '<symbol><low>-<symbol><high>' with no spaces around the dash. symbol mapping is USDвҶ’'$', GBPвҶ’'ВЈ', EURвҶ’'вӮ¬', and each option figures out its own symbol from its currencyCode field independently. so for example, USD with low=10, high=14 renders as '$10-$14'. same rule applies across the board, with the currency symbol resolution logic in the fare-range formatter living under src/formatters/ in the CurrencyFormatter/similar module.

also confirming the shared formatting rules: duration humanization is the same in both views, so the price table duration column and the wait-time table time column both use the same function. less than 60 seconds renders as '<n> sec.' like '4 sec.' or '30 sec.'. exactly divisible by 60 with no remainder renders as '<n> min.' like '5 min.' or '10 min.'. if there are both minutes and remaining seconds, render '<m> min. <s> sec.' like '1 min. 30 sec.'. thatвҖҷs the same behavior you see with 300s вҶ’ '5 min.' and also with 30s, 60s, 90s. distance is similar in that it should stay consistent too: '<value> mi.' for MILE and '<value> km.' for KILOMETER, with the numeric value rounded to at most two decimal places. for example value=1, unit=MILE renders as '1 mi.' and value=9, unit=MILE renders as '9 mi.'.

last thing is structure and error handling. we really do want this organized under a src/ tree with separate units for value objects for the ride option data models, formatters for currency localization, distance formatting, duration humanization, and row grouping, plus table builders for the price table renderer and wait-time table renderer. the execution adapter for stdin/stdout + JSON parsing needs to stay completely separate from the core rendering logic, and we should not collapse everything into a single file. and for bad inputs, whether thatвҖҷs unknown mode selectors, unknown distance units, or malformed payloads, please surface a normalized, language-neutral error category instead of leaking host runtime fault details like stack traces or exception class names. the execution adapter should catch those and return a clean error string.

trigger_keywords: how products are grouped in time table
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the wait-time table, products sharing the same estimateSeconds value are collapsed into a single body row. Their names are joined with commas (no spaces around commas) in the order they appear in the input array. Distinct wait times produce separate rows, ordered by first-encounter position in the input. For example if 'jae' and 'bae' both have estimateSeconds=1, the products cell shows 'jae,bae'.

trigger_keywords: what color are the table borders
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All box-drawing border characters (в”Ң, в”Җ, в”¬, в”җ, в”Ӯ, в”ң, в”ј, в”Ө, в””, в”ҙ, в”ҳ) are individually wrapped with ANSI gray escape codes: \u001b[90m before each border segment and \u001b[39m after it. Cell content between borders is NOT colored. This applies to both the price table and the wait-time table.

trigger_keywords: start end location in price table
location rows at bottom of price table
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The price table ends with two footer rows below the last option row. The first footer row shows a рҹ“Қ pin pictograph in the left cell and the start location name in a merged right cell spanning the remaining four data columns. The second footer row shows a рҹ”ҡ pictograph in the left cell and the end location name in the same merged right cell. The separator above the first footer row uses в”ј and в”ҙ characters to close the upper portion of the data columns while keeping the left column open.

trigger_keywords: how the location name is displayed at top
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wait-time table has a full-width banner as its very first row (above the column headers). This banner spans both columns and centers 'рҹ“Қ <locationName>' within the full inner width of the table. The two-column pictograph header row (вҸі on the left, рҹҡҳ on the right) appears below this banner, separated by a в”ңв”¬в”Ө junction row.

Column widths are determined by the widest cell content in each column across all rows (including the header pictograph row). Each cell is padded with one space on the left and enough trailing spaces on the right to fill to the column width, then one more space. The surge column in the price table always has a minimum inner width of 10 to accommodate ' рҹ’Ҙ Surgeрҹ’Ҙ '. Pictograph characters count as 2 display-width characters each.

trigger_keywords: what icons appear in price table header
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The price table has five columns with these header pictographs (centered with padding): рҹҡҳ for product name, рҹ’ё for fare range, рҹ”ғ for distance, вҸі for duration, and 'рҹ’Ҙ Surgeрҹ’Ҙ' for the surge indicator. The wait-time table two-column header uses вҸі for the time column and рҹҡҳ for the products column.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) вҖ” never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only вҖ” NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic вҖ” every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

show me feature1 1 full price table test cases
what are the feature1 1 full price table test inputs
give me the feature1_1_full_price_table test file
feature1 1 full price table expected output
what does feature1 1 full price table expect

show me feature1 2 surge indicator test cases

what does feature1 2 surge indicator expect
test cases for feature1 3 currency range
show me feature1 3 currency range test cases

what does feature1 3 currency range expect
test cases for feature2 1 grouped time table
show me feature2 1 grouped time table test cases

feature2 1 grouped time table expected output
what does feature2 1 grouped time table expect
test cases for feature2 2 distinct time table

what are the feature2 2 distinct time table test inputs
give me the feature2_2_distinct_time_table test file
feature2 2 distinct time table expected output
what does feature2 2 distinct time table expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: that gray styling we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: ANSI escape code \u001b[90m (gray foreground) applied before each individual box-drawing border character or segment, closed with \u001b[39m (reset foreground) immediately after. Cell text content is never wrapped in color codes. Confirmed by examining the raw expected_output strings in any test case JSON file.

context_pointers.vague_reference: not just one big file dumping everything to stdout
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architectural NFR #1 and #2 from start.md: the execution adapter (JSON parsing + stdout writing) must be a separate layer from the core rendering library. The core API accepts a structured value object and returns a string; it has no direct dependency on process.stdin, process.stdout, or JSON.parse.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have documented on my end вҖ” you'll want to dig into the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at the expected_output strings directly to reverse-engineer the exact behavior. The test cases are the ground truth for any formatting question I can't answer offhand.
